message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 23 (18 todo)
Recent changes: Changed loyalty from a multiplier (premiumWithSurcharges*0.8) to a linear gatedPolicyBaseModifier-style subtraction of policyBasePremium*0.20 gated on yearsWithMHPCO>=2. Now loyalty, firstInsurance, and followUp are all linear percentages of policyBasePremium. Both integration examples (165, 160) now pass.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Now that all three policy-wide modifiers (loyalty -20%, firstInsurance +10%, followUp -15%) share the gated-policy-base-modifier structure, evaluate consolidating them and ensuring the quote() pipeline reads clearly.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 23 tests pass. Now let me work through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The recent change made loyalty, firstInsurance, and followUp all linear percentages of `policyBasePremium`, all flowing through `gatedPolicyBaseModifier`. Let me evaluate the names and structure.

**Naming Evaluation**:
- `gatedPolicyBaseModifier`: This name reveals intent well — it computes a modifier off the policy base premium, gated by a boolean condition. Good name. Keep.
- `loyaltyDiscount`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `followUpDiscount`: These reveal intent clearly. Keep.
- The `quote()` return line is dense: `premiumWithSurcharges - loyaltyDiscount + firstInsuranceSurcharge - followUpDiscount + PROCESSING_FEE`. This mixes additions and subtractions of differently-named modifiers, which obscures the structure.

The user asked to **evaluate consolidating** the three gated-policy-base modifiers and **ensure the quote() pipeline reads clearly**.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass of `quote()`

```
export function quote(input: QuoteInput): number {
  const policyBasePremium = policyBasePremiumFor(input.items);
  const premiumWithSurcharges = policyBasePremium + itemSurchargesFor(input.items);
  const loyaltyDiscount = gatedPolicyBaseModifier(input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD, policyBasePremium, LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE);
  const firstInsuranceSurcharge = gatedPolicyBaseModifier(input.firstInsurance, policyBasePremium, FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE);
  const followUpDiscount = gatedPolicyBaseModifier(input.followUp, policyBasePremium, FOLLOW_UP_DISCOUNT_RATE);
  return Math.ceil(premiumWithSurcharges - loyaltyDiscount + firstInsuranceSurcharge - followUpDiscount + PROCESSING_FEE);
}
```

Component count:
- Bindings: `input`, `policyBasePremium`, `premiumWithSurcharges`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `followUpDiscount`, three rate constants referenced, threshold = approx 12 bindings = 12
- Invocations: `policyBasePremiumFor`, `itemSurchargesFor`, `gatedPolicyBaseModifier` ×3, `Math.ceil` = 6 invocations = 12
- Arithmetic operators in return (`-`, `+`, `+`, `-`, `+` = 5; plus `+` in surcharges line, `+` in comparison `>=`) — treating operators as part of expression mass
- Conditionals: `>=` comparison = 1 = 4

The key observation: the three modifiers differ only by sign convention. Loyalty and followUp are subtracted; firstInsurance is added. This sign-juggling in the return is the readability problem.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)** is the priority here. The consolidation opportunity: represent each policy-wide modifier as a signed adjustment to the premium, so they can be summed uniformly. A discount is just a negative surcharge. This unifies the three into a single list-driven sum, removing the asymmetric `- ... + ... -` structure.

Let me consolidate. I'll model the three policy-wide modifiers as signed rates (discounts negative, surcharges positive) so they all add into the premium uniformly.

content.text:
Type-check passes cleanly with no errors.

## Step 5: APP Mass Comparison

**Before** — three separate `gatedPolicyBaseModifier` invocations plus an asymmetric `- ... + ... -` return expression:
- 3 invocations of `gatedPolicyBaseModifier` = 6
- 5 arithmetic operators in the return + `Math.ceil` invocation
- 1 conditional inside `gatedPolicyBaseModifier`
- 3 intermediate bindings (`loyaltyDiscount`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `followUpDiscount`)

**After** — one `policyWideModifiersFor` invocation, a single `+ ... + ...` return, with the gating folded into one loop:
- `quote()` return: 2 additions + `Math.ceil` invocation (no sign-juggling)
- Modifier logic consolidated into one loop (mass 5) + one conditional (mass 4) inside the helper, replacing three separate gated invocations

The `quote()` body dropped from 6 bindings/3 modifier invocations to 3 bindings/1 modifier invocation. The return expression went from 5 signed operators to 2 uniform additions. Net mass of `quote()` itself is meaningfully lower; the helper absorbs one loop+conditional in place of the previous three-call expansion, keeping overall mass roughly flat while concentrating complexity in one well-named place.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied** — Consolidated the three policy-wide modifiers (Rule 2 + Rule 3):

- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The `quote()` return changed from `premiumWithSurcharges - loyaltyDiscount + firstInsuranceSurcharge - followUpDiscount + PROCESSING_FEE` to `premiumWithSurcharges + policyWideModifiers + PROCESSING_FEE`. The reader no longer has to track which modifiers subtract and which add — the sign is now encoded once, declaratively, in each rate constant (discounts negative, surcharges positive). The pipeline reads as a clear three-line story: base premium with item surcharges, plus policy-wide modifiers, plus fee, rounded up.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): The three structurally-identical `gatedPolicyBaseModifier(...)` calls — which differed only by gate condition, rate, and sign — collapsed into a single data-driven `policyWideModifiersFor` helper iterating one list of `{ active, rate }` entries. Adding or removing a policy-wide modifier is now a one-line data change rather than a new binding plus a new term in the return expression.
- `gatedPolicyBaseModifier` was removed since its sole role (conditionally multiply base by rate) is now the body of the consolidation loop.

**Why this over alternatives**: Encoding discount/surcharge as the sign of the rate is the key simplification — it lets all three modifiers be summed uniformly, which is what removes the error-prone `- ... + ... -` asymmetry. Clarity (Rule 2) drove the choice; the data-list form (Rule 3) is a natural consequence.

## Step 7: Completion Report

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Consolidated three policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first-insurance, follow-up) into a single data-driven `policyWideModifiersFor` helper; encoded discount-vs-surcharge as the sign of each rate constant; removed the now-redundant `gatedPolicyBaseModifier`.
- **Mass Change**: `quote()` body reduced from 6 bindings / 3 modifier invocations / 5 signed return operators to 3 bindings / 1 invocation / 2 uniform additions. Overall mass roughly flat (one loop+conditional absorbs the three former calls) while complexity is concentrated in one named, extensible helper.
- **Tests**: All 23 passing ✅ (18 todo remain). Type-check clean.

- Tests: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_06-12-03_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`
